L1.area: QUIC connection lifecycle and handshake state management

L2.description: A nil pointer dereference vulnerability exists where a specially ordered sequence of frames within a handshake packet causes the protocol implementation to prematurely release internal state. When subsequent frames in the same packet attempt to access this already-released state, a nil pointer dereference occurs, leading to a panic that crashes the node. An attacker can trigger this with minimal effort, as it only requires completing the QUIC handshake.

L3.area: QUIC connection lifecycle and handshake state management
L3.description: A nil pointer dereference vulnerability exists where a specially ordered sequence of frames within a handshake packet causes the protocol implementation to prematurely release internal state. When subsequent frames in the same packet attempt to access this already-released state, a nil pointer dereference occurs, leading to a panic that crashes the node. An attacker can trigger this with minimal effort, as it only requires completing the QUIC handshake.

ground_truth.reason: When the QUIC handshake completes, the Handshake packet number space is dropped immediately. If an ACK frame is serialized after the CRYPTO frame in the same packet, processing that ACK attempts to access the already-dropped packet number space, causing a nil pointer dereference and panic.
